posts.title: Claude Code replaced my entire dev workflow
posts.hook:
I mass-deleted 14 browser tabs last Tuesday.

Not because I was done with them. Because I realized I hadn't needed them in three weeks.
posts.body:
I mass-deleted 14 browser tabs last Tuesday.

Not because I was done with them. Because I realized I hadn't needed them in three weeks.

Stack Overflow. MDN. Random GitHub issues. Three separate Tailwind docs tabs. All replaced by one thing: an AI agent inside my terminal.

I've been running Claude Code as my primary dev environment for about 60 days now. Not as a copilot. As the actual builder. I describe what I want, it writes the code, runs the tests, fixes the errors, and commits.

The shift that matters isn't "AI writes code faster." It's that the feedback loop collapsed.

Old loop: write code, Google error, read docs, try fix, repeat.
New loop: describe intent, review output, ship.

Some real numbers from my last week:
- 4 Next.js pages built and deployed
- 1 Python scraping pipeline refactored
- 2 new API integrations
- Total time I spent writing code manually: maybe 90 minutes

The uncomfortable part nobody talks about... I'm mass-producing working software and my typing speed is irrelevant now. What matters is taste. Knowing what to build, what to skip, and when the output is wrong.

The new developer skill stack is curation, not creation.
posts.cta: What's one thing AI agents handle for you now that you used to do manually? Genuinely curious how others are adapting.

Critiques current tools and proposes optimizations for developer workflows in agent-based systems. Predicts a shift towards agent-native infrastructure in the coming months.

remixes.label: before-after-workflow
remixes.hook: My dev workflow six months ago: Google it, scan three Stack Overflow answers, copy-paste, pray it works, debug for 20 minutes when it doesn't.
remixes.body:
My dev workflow six months ago: Google it, scan three Stack Overflow answers, copy-paste, pray it works, debug for 20 minutes when it doesn't.

My dev workflow now: describe what I need in plain English, watch it get built, review the output, ship.

I didn't mass-adopt some fancy new IDE. I started treating Claude Code as my primary builder instead of my assistant. The shift happened gradually. First it was "help me write this function." Then "build this entire component." Then "read the codebase, find the bug, fix it, and run the tests."

The moment it clicked was when I realized I hadn't opened documentation in weeks. Not because I memorized everything. Because the agent already knows the APIs, the patterns, the edge cases. It reads the docs so I don't have to.

What actually changed: I spend 80% less time context-switching between browser tabs and terminal. I catch architectural mistakes earlier because I can describe what I want at a higher level. And I ship roughly 3x more features per week, not because I type faster, but because I eliminated the research loop entirely.

The uncomfortable part? I'm mass-deleting skills I spent years building. The ability to speed-read documentation. The muscle memory for specific API signatures. Those skills aren't differentiators anymore. Taste, architecture decisions, and knowing what to build are.
remixes.cta: What skill did you realize AI made obsolete for you? Not in theory. One you actually stopped using.

remixes.label: terminal-native-thesis
remixes.hook: The browser was never a good developer tool. We just didn't have an alternative.
remixes.body:
The browser was never a good developer tool. We just didn't have an alternative.

Think about the actual workflow. You're coding in a terminal or editor. You hit a problem. You leave your context, open a browser, type a query, scan results, find something promising, read it, translate it back to your specific problem, switch back to your editor, and implement.

That's six context switches for one answer.

I've been running an AI agent directly in my terminal for two months. No browser needed. When I hit a problem, I describe it where I'm already working. The agent reads my actual code, understands my actual context, and gives me an answer that fits my actual codebase.

The difference isn't speed, though it's faster. The difference is that I never leave my flow state. I stay in the terminal the entire session. The agent handles research, implementation, testing, even git operations.

Last week I noticed something absurd. I had 14 browser tabs open from three weeks ago. Stack Overflow, MDN, GitHub issues. All untouched. I closed them all.

The browser era of development is ending. Not because browsers are bad. Because the terminal finally got smart enough to replace the parts we were using browsers for.
remixes.cta: How much of your browser usage during coding is actually necessary vs. habitual? I'd bet most of it is habit.

posts.title: GTM engineers are the new 10x hires
posts.hook:
Every sales team I talk to has the same problem.

They have 8 reps, 3 tools, and zero people who can wire them together.
posts.body:
Every sales team I talk to has the same problem.

They have 8 reps, 3 tools, and zero people who can wire them together.

CRM has 40,000 records. Outbound tool sends sequences. Enrichment tool pulls firmographics. But nothing talks to anything else. So reps still manually copy-paste job titles from LinkedIn into Salesforce.

This is why GTM engineering exists.

Not as a buzzword. As a function. Someone who can take Clay, your CRM, your outbound platform, and your enrichment stack and build the pipes between them.

I used to be the SDR doing 200+ sends a day off primary domains with zero warmup. That was the job. Now I build systems that do what I used to do, except they run at 3am while I sleep and they never fat-finger a merge field.

The companies winning outbound right now aren't hiring more reps. They're hiring one person who can build the automation layer across their entire stack.

One GTM engineer replaces the output of 3-4 manual processes. Not by working harder. By connecting what already exists.

If your sales ops team can't write a Clay formula or build a webhook, you're leaving pipeline on the table.
posts.cta: Are you seeing this role emerge on your team, or is ops still duct-taping tools together manually?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering with sales processes. Shares a framework for automating outbound at scale while maintaining personalization. Emphasizes data-driven iteration for revenue growth.

remixes.label: job-posting-that-doesnt-exist
remixes.hook:
I searched LinkedIn for "GTM Engineer" last year. Got 200 results.

I searched again last week. Over 4,000.
remixes.body:
I searched LinkedIn for "GTM Engineer" last year. Got 200 results.

I searched again last week. Over 4,000.

This role is emerging fast, and most companies still don't know they need it.

Here's the pattern I keep seeing. A sales team has a CRM, an outbound tool, an enrichment platform, and maybe an intent data provider. Four tools. Zero integration. So reps spend half their day doing manual data entry that should be automated.

The VP of Sales blames the reps. The reps blame the tools. Nobody blames the architecture, because nobody owns it.

That's the gap a GTM engineer fills. Not another ops person managing dashboards. A builder who can write code, connect APIs, build automations, and create a system where data flows from enrichment to CRM to outbound without a human copying and pasting.

The companies hiring for this role are seeing 30-50% increases in outbound efficiency. Not from better copy or more reps. From eliminating the manual glue work between tools.

The ones that aren't hiring for it? They're adding headcount to compensate for broken infrastructure. Hiring two more SDRs instead of one engineer who could make the existing eight actually productive.

This isn't a trend. It's a correction. Sales teams have been under-engineered for a decade.
remixes.cta: Does your org have someone who owns the technical layer of your GTM stack? Or is it still "the ops person who's good at Zapier"?

remixes.label: cost-of-no-gtm-engineer
remixes.hook:
Quick math on what broken sales infrastructure actually costs.

8 reps. 30 minutes per day on manual data work. That's 20 hours per week of engineering work disguised as sales activity.
remixes.body:
Quick math on what broken sales infrastructure actually costs.

8 reps. 30 minutes per day on manual data work. That's 20 hours per week of engineering work disguised as sales activity.

At an average SDR cost of $75K loaded, that's roughly $36K per year in wasted salary. Across 8 reps. Just from copy-pasting between tools.

I did this math for a client last month. They had reps manually enriching leads in LinkedIn, copying job titles into Salesforce, checking company websites for technographic signals, and formatting sequences by hand. Every single one of those tasks had an API that could automate it.

The total wasted labor: over $280K annually. The cost of the GTM engineer who fixed it: $140K.

This is why I keep saying GTM engineering isn't optional anymore. It's not a nice-to-have innovation hire. It's the person who stops your $75K-per-head sales team from doing $20/hour data entry.

The role sits at the intersection of sales ops, data engineering, and automation. They own the pipes between your tools. They build the workflows that turn raw data into qualified, enriched, routed leads without a human touching them.

Every week without this role is another week your reps are the integration layer. And they're the most expensive integration layer you could possibly build.
remixes.cta: Run this math on your own team. Hours per rep per day on manual data work, times headcount, times loaded cost. The number will bother you.

posts.title: Edge AI is a distraction for most teams
posts.hook: Hot take: 95% of teams deploying AI "at the edge" don't need to be at the edge.
posts.body:
Hot take: 95% of teams deploying AI "at the edge" don't need to be at the edge.

I see this pattern constantly. Team reads a blog post about low-latency AI inference. Gets excited. Spends two weeks configuring edge functions with model routing. Ships it. Realizes their actual bottleneck was a 3-second database query that runs regardless of where the AI lives.

Edge deployment matters when you're serving millions of requests where 50ms latency difference changes conversion. That's Vercel. That's Cloudflare. That's maybe 200 companies.

For the rest of us building internal tools, GTM automation, content pipelines... a normal serverless function with a good caching strategy is more than enough.

I run AI agents that generate blog posts, score leads, enrich CRM records, and draft outbound sequences. Every single one runs on standard compute. Not edge. Not GPU clusters. Regular functions.

The infrastructure conversation is a trap for builders. You can spend a month optimizing where your model runs. Or you can spend that month building the thing that actually makes money.

Ship on the simplest infrastructure that works. Optimize when you have the traffic that demands it.

Most don't. Most won't.
posts.cta: What's the most over-engineered AI deployment you've seen (or built)?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Talks about deploying AI agents at edge with Next.js and Vercel AI SDK, focusing on low-latency automation for web apps. Details infrastructure challenges in serverless AI and new tools for developers to build agentic UIs. Notes recent benchmarks showing 3x speedups.

remixes.label: premature-optimization-angle
remixes.hook: The most expensive AI decision I see teams make isn't choosing the wrong model. It's optimizing for the wrong bottleneck.
remixes.body:
The most expensive AI decision I see teams make isn't choosing the wrong model. It's optimizing for the wrong bottleneck.

I watched a team spend three weeks moving their AI inference to edge functions. They benchmarked the latency improvement: 40ms saved on the model call. Impressive, right?

Except their API route also made a database query that took 2,800ms. And a third-party enrichment call that took 1,200ms. The AI inference was already the fastest part of the pipeline.

They optimized the thing that didn't matter while ignoring the thing that did.

This is premature optimization applied to AI infrastructure, and it's everywhere right now. Teams read about edge AI, get excited about the architecture, and implement it without profiling their actual bottleneck first.

The fix is boring. Before you deploy anything to the edge, run a trace on your full request lifecycle. Find the slowest segment. Fix that first. In most cases, it's a database query, an external API call, or an N+1 problem in your data fetching layer.

Edge AI makes sense when sub-100ms response times matter and your AI inference is genuinely the bottleneck. For a real-time game. A trading platform. A safety system.

For your SaaS dashboard that runs a sentiment analysis call? The edge isn't your problem. Your unindexed Postgres query is.
remixes.cta: Before your next infra decision: when's the last time you actually profiled the full request to find the real bottleneck?

remixes.label: complexity-budget-framework
remixes.hook: Every engineering team has a complexity budget. Edge AI blows through it for almost no return.
remixes.body:
Every engineering team has a complexity budget. Edge AI blows through it for almost no return.

Here's what I mean. Your team has a finite amount of complexity it can manage before things start breaking. Every architectural decision either spends or saves from that budget.

Edge AI deployment costs a lot of complexity budget. You need model routing. Regional function configuration. Cold start management. Cache invalidation strategies. Monitoring across distributed nodes. Fallback logic when edge locations go down.

What do you get for all that complexity? Usually 30-80ms of latency reduction on the inference step.

Meanwhile, moving your database to a connection pooler: 2 hours of work, 500ms saved. Adding a cache layer on your most-hit endpoint: half a day, 2 seconds saved. Fixing that N+1 query in your dashboard: 1 hour, 3 seconds saved.

Simple changes, massive impact, minimal complexity cost.

I've talked to over a dozen teams that deployed AI at the edge this year. Exactly two of them needed to. The rest would have gotten better results from a database index and a Redis cache.

The question isn't "can we run AI at the edge?" It's "does the latency gain justify the complexity cost?" For 95% of teams, the answer is no. Spend your complexity budget on things that move the needle.
remixes.cta: What's the highest-complexity, lowest-impact technical decision you've seen a team make recently?

I see this pattern constantly. Team reads a blog post about low-latency AI inference. Gets excited. Spends two weeks configuring edge functions with model routing. Ships it. Realizes their actual bottleneck was a 3-second database query that runs regardless of where the AI lives.

Here's what I'd recommend before making any edge deployment decision.

First, profile your full request lifecycle. Not just the AI inference step. The entire request from client to response. Identify your actual bottleneck. In my experience, it's almost always a database query, an external API call, or a data fetching inefficiency.

Second, calculate your complexity cost. Edge deployment introduces model routing, regional configuration, cold start management, and distributed monitoring. That's significant operational overhead. Make sure the latency gain justifies it.

Third, exhaust the simple wins first. Connection pooling, query optimization, caching layers, CDN configuration. These typically deliver 10-50x more latency improvement per hour of engineering effort than edge migration.

Edge AI deployment makes sense in specific scenarios: real-time applications where sub-100ms matters, user-facing inference where geographic latency is the proven bottleneck, and privacy-sensitive workloads that need data locality.

For everything else, I'd recommend keeping your AI in a standard serverless function and investing that engineering time in optimizing the parts of your stack that actually cause the slowdown.

Hot take: 95% of teams deploying AI "at the edge" don't need to be at the edge.

But they're doing it anyway because it sounds cool in architecture reviews.

Let's be honest about what's happening here. Edge AI became a status symbol. It's the engineering equivalent of driving a sports car to the grocery store. Technically capable. Practically pointless.

I watched a team burn two full sprints on edge deployment. Custom model routing. Regional function configs. Cold start optimization. Monitoring dashboards for 12 edge locations. Real engineering work. Impressive stuff.

Then I looked at their request waterfall. The AI inference took 120ms. The database query behind it took 3,100ms. They optimized the part that was already fast and ignored the part that was actually slow.

This is what happens when engineering teams optimize for resume-driven development instead of user experience. "Deployed AI at the edge across 15 regions" looks amazing on LinkedIn. "Added a database index that cut page load by 3 seconds" does not.

But guess which one your users actually notice?

The edge AI hype is a distraction from the boring, effective work that most teams should be doing. Fix your queries. Add caching. Optimize your data fetching. These aren't exciting conference talks. They're the actual performance improvements.

Stop building impressive architectures that solve imaginary problems. Start profiling your requests and fixing the real ones.

posts.title: Solo builders are outshipping funded teams
posts.hook:
A solo dev shipped 4 products last quarter.

A 12-person startup shipped a landing page redesign.
posts.body:
A solo dev shipped 4 products last quarter.

A 12-person startup shipped a landing page redesign.

remixes.label: coordination-tax-breakdown
remixes.hook: I calculated the coordination tax on a 12-person startup last week. 47% of their engineering hours went to meetings, alignment, and context-sharing. Not building.
remixes.body:
I calculated the coordination tax on a 12-person startup last week. 47% of their engineering hours went to meetings, alignment, and context-sharing. Not building.

Here's the breakdown. 4 hours/week in standups. 3 hours/week in sprint planning and retros. 6 hours/week in cross-team syncs because the frontend team needs context from the backend team. 2 hours/week writing and reading PRD updates. Plus the invisible cost: 15-20 minutes of context-switching after every meeting.

That's roughly 19 hours per person per week not spent building. Out of a 40-hour week.

Meanwhile, the solo dev I know ships every single day. Not because they work more hours. Because every hour is a building hour. No meetings. No alignment. No waiting for someone else to unblock them.

When they hit a technical decision, they make it in 30 seconds and move on. When a funded team hits the same decision, it becomes a Slack thread, then a meeting, then a document, then another meeting.

I'm not saying teams are bad. Complex products need coordination. But I am saying most startups wildly underestimate what coordination actually costs.

The solo builders winning right now aren't 10x developers. They're 1x developers keeping 100% of their time. The funded teams have 10x developers keeping 53% of theirs.

Do the math on which one ships more.
remixes.cta: If you're on a team: track how many hours you actually build this week vs. how many go to coordination. The ratio might surprise you.

remixes.label: leverage-stack-angle
remixes.hook: The solo dev advantage isn't talent. It's leverage stacking.
remixes.body:
The solo dev advantage isn't talent. It's leverage stacking.

Every tool that eliminates a category of work gives a solo builder disproportionate advantage over a team. Because when you remove a task from a team, you save one person's time. When you remove it from a solo builder, you remove an entire bottleneck.

Here's what the leverage stack looks like in 2026.

AI agents handle 70% of implementation work. Not autocomplete. Full feature builds from description to tests to commit. That eliminates the need for junior developers on most projects.

Managed infrastructure handles deployment, scaling, and monitoring. That eliminates the need for a DevOps person. CI/CD pipelines run from a config file.

Design systems and component libraries handle 80% of UI decisions. That eliminates the need for a designer on most features. You compose from proven patterns instead of designing from scratch.

Payment processing, auth, email, analytics. All API calls now. Each one used to be a person's job for a week.

Stack all of those together and one person with good taste and the right tools can build what took a team of 8 three years ago.

The funded teams aren't obsolete. They're necessary for certain kinds of products. But the threshold for "needs a team" keeps moving up. Products that required 12 people in 2023 need 3 in 2026. Products that needed 3 can be built solo.

The builders who see this are stacking leverage. The ones who don't are stacking headcount.

posts.title: Your outbound is broken because your data is
posts.hook: I audited a client's outbound last month. 12,000 contacts in their sequence tool. 9,400 had wrong job titles.
posts.body:
I audited a client's outbound last month. 12,000 contacts in their sequence tool. 9,400 had wrong job titles.

Not slightly wrong. "Marketing Manager" when the person had been VP of Revenue for 8 months. "Software Engineer" for someone who'd left the company entirely.

They were blaming their copy. Their sequences. Their reps. The problem was upstream.

This is the dirty secret of outbound automation. You can have the best sequences, the sharpest personalization, the most dialed-in ICP. If the data underneath is stale, none of it matters.

Here's what fixed it:

1. Clay waterfall enrichment on every contact before it enters a sequence. Not after. Before.
2. Title normalization formula that maps 200+ job title variations to 15 ICP buckets
3. Company employee count + funding stage verified at sequence entry, not at list build (those numbers change)
4. Automated bounce + reply signal feeding back to update the source record

Result after 30 days: reply rate went from 1.8% to 6.2%. Same copy. Same sequences. Same reps.

The difference was sending to people who actually match your ICP with current information.

Stop optimizing your email copy. Start auditing your data.
posts.cta: When's the last time you actually spot-checked the accuracy of your outbound list?

remixes.label: data-decay-timeline
remixes.hook:
Your outbound list loses 3% accuracy per month. By month six, nearly 1 in 5 records is wrong.

Most teams don't check until month twelve.
remixes.body:
Your outbound list loses 3% accuracy per month. By month six, nearly 1 in 5 records is wrong.

Most teams don't check until month twelve.

I track data decay across client outbound lists as part of my GTM engineering work. The pattern is remarkably consistent. A freshly enriched list starts at 90-95% accuracy. People change jobs, get promoted, leave companies. Titles drift. Emails bounce. Companies get acquired or restructured.

At 3 months, you're at roughly 85% accuracy. Workable. Your sequences still mostly land.

At 6 months, you're at 82%. Bounce rates start climbing. Reply rates dip. Reps start complaining that "the leads are bad."

At 12 months, you're below 70%. You're emailing people who left the company 8 months ago. Using titles that are two promotions out of date. Pitching to companies that merged or pivoted entirely.

And here's the thing: nobody is looking at this. The outbound list sits in the sequence tool and nobody validates it. Teams will A/B test subject lines, rewrite copy five times, and rebuild their entire cadence structure before anyone thinks to check whether the data underneath is still accurate.

The fix isn't complicated. Run a re-enrichment pass on your active lists every 90 days. Flag records where the job title, company, or email has changed. Remove records that can't be verified. It takes a few hours with the right tooling.

But those few hours will do more for your reply rates than any copy rewrite ever will.
remixes.cta: When's the last time you re-enriched your active outbound list? If you can't remember, it's been too long.

remixes.label: blame-the-wrong-layer
remixes.hook: Your sales team spent Q1 rewriting email copy. The problem was never the copy.
remixes.body:
Your sales team spent Q1 rewriting email copy. The problem was never the copy.

I see this cycle every quarter. Reply rates drop. Leadership says "fix the messaging." Marketing rewrites the sequences. SDR managers run copy workshops. Someone buys a course on cold email frameworks.

Reply rates don't improve.

So they blame the reps. "You're not personalizing enough." "You need to research before you send." "Try a different opening line."

Reply rates still don't improve.

Nobody checks the data. I checked it for a client last month. 12,000 contacts in their sequence tool. 9,400 had wrong job titles. Not slightly wrong. "Marketing Manager" when the person had been VP of Revenue for 8 months. "Software Engineer" for someone who'd left the company entirely.

They'd been sending perfectly crafted emails to completely wrong people. For months.

This is the outbound debugging hierarchy most teams get backwards. They start at the top, messaging and copy, and work down. They should start at the bottom.

Layer 1: Is the data accurate? (Check this first.)
Layer 2: Are we targeting the right personas? (Check this second.)
Layer 3: Is the timing right? (Check this third.)
Layer 4: Is the copy good? (Check this last.)

In my experience, 70% of underperforming outbound programs have a Layer 1 or Layer 2 problem. They never reach the point where copy matters because they're emailing the wrong people with the wrong titles at the wrong companies.
remixes.cta: Next time reply rates drop, start debugging from the data layer up. Not from the copy down. What layer is your team's actual bottleneck?

posts.title: AI agents need debugging tools not more frameworks
posts.hook:
We have 47 AI agent frameworks.

We have approximately 2 good ways to debug them when they break.
posts.body:
We have 47 AI agent frameworks.

We have approximately 2 good ways to debug them when they break.

I've been building AI agents for production workflows for months now. Blog generators, lead scoring, content pipelines, CRM enrichment. The building part is honestly solved. Pick a framework, wire up your tools, ship it.

The debugging part is where everyone's stuck.

When an agent makes a bad decision on step 7 of a 12-step workflow, how do you figure out why? When it hallucinates a field value that cascades through your pipeline, how do you trace it?

Right now my debugging process looks like this: dump the full conversation log, ctrl+F through 4,000 lines of JSON, find the step where it went sideways, guess at why, adjust the prompt, re-run, hope.

That's not engineering. That's archaeology.

What we actually need:
- Step-level observability with decision traces
- Diff views between expected and actual agent behavior
- Replay capabilities so you can re-run from the failure point
- Cost attribution per step, not just per run

Addy Osmani is right that browser devtools thinking needs to come to AI. We solved "why is my web app slow" with Chrome DevTools. We haven't solved "why did my agent do that" yet.

The next wave of AI tooling isn't more ways to build agents. It's better ways to understand them when they fail.
posts.cta: How are you debugging your AI agents right now? Logs and prayer, or something better?

inspired_by.author: Addy Osmani
inspired_by.summary: Reviews performance tools for AI agent debugging in browsers, bridging web dev and AI automation. Discusses infrastructure needs for running agents client-side without compromising speed. Provides code snippets for integrating with modern dev stacks.

remixes.label: observability-gap
remixes.hook:
The AI agent industry has a dirty secret.

Nobody can tell you why their agent did what it did five minutes ago.
remixes.body:
The AI agent industry has a dirty secret.

Nobody can tell you why their agent did what it did five minutes ago.

I run agents in production. Blog generators, lead scoring, CRM enrichment. They work great until they don't. And when they don't, you're staring at a 200-line trace of LLM calls trying to figure out which tool call went sideways at 2am.

We built incredible observability for web apps. Datadog, Sentry, Honeycomb. You can trace a single HTTP request through 14 microservices. But an AI agent that made a bad decision? Good luck. Your options are print statements and reading raw JSON.

The gap is wild. We have more frameworks for building agents than we have tools for understanding them. LangSmith exists. Braintrust exists. But the ecosystem is where web observability was in 2012.

What I actually want: replayable agent runs. Give me a timeline view. Show me every tool call, every LLM decision, every branching point. Let me scrub through it like a video. Let me fork from any decision point and ask "what if the model had chosen differently here?"

The teams that figure out agent observability first will own the next wave of infrastructure tooling. Building agents is a solved problem. Understanding them is wide open.
remixes.cta: What's the worst agent debugging experience you've had? I want to hear the horror stories.

remixes.label: production-war-stories
remixes.hook:
My AI agent sent the wrong enrichment data to 340 leads last Tuesday.

It took me 4 hours to figure out why. Here's what I learned.
remixes.body:
My AI agent sent the wrong enrichment data to 340 leads last Tuesday.

It took me 4 hours to figure out why. Here's what I learned.

The agent runs nightly. Pulls new leads, enriches company data with Claude, scores them, pushes to CRM. It's been stable for weeks. Then Tuesday's batch came through with hallucinated revenue numbers for half the companies.

Debugging process: I checked the logs. The logs said "enrichment complete, 340 leads processed." Thanks, logs. Very helpful. I checked the raw API responses. All looked fine structurally. I checked the prompt. Unchanged.

Turns out a upstream data source changed their API response format slightly. One nested field moved. The agent didn't error. It just started filling in blanks with confident nonsense.

Three things I added after this:

1. Output validation on every agent step. Not just "did it return JSON" but "does this revenue number exist in any source data?"
2. Diff alerts. If Tuesday's output looks wildly different from Monday's, flag it before pushing to CRM.
3. A "dry run" mode that processes 10 leads and shows me results before running the full batch.

None of this came from a framework. It's all custom. And that's the problem. Every team building agents is reinventing the same safety nets from scratch.
remixes.cta: What's your worst AI agent production incident? No judgment. We've all been there.

posts.title: Content strategy is outbound in disguise
posts.hook:
The best outbound email I sent last quarter wasn't an email.

It was a LinkedIn post that my prospect's VP of Sales shared with their team.
posts.body:
The best outbound email I sent last quarter wasn't an email.

It was a LinkedIn post that my prospect's VP of Sales shared with their team.

Here's what happened. I wrote a tactical breakdown of a Clay enrichment workflow. Specific columns, specific formulas, specific use case. A VP at a target account shared it internally. Their ops lead DM'd me asking if I could build something similar.

No cold email. No sequence. No "bumping this to the top of your inbox." Just useful content that reached the right person through organic distribution.

This isn't a "content marketing" play. It's outbound with a longer fuse and better targeting.

The math: a cold email has maybe a 3% open rate from a new sender. A LinkedIn post seen by your ICP has 100% "open rate" because they chose to read it. The intent is completely different.

I now treat my content calendar as a parallel outbound channel. Every post is written for a specific ICP segment. Every tactical breakdown targets a pain point I know my target accounts have.

The posts that drive pipeline aren't the ones about "the future of GTM." They're the ones showing exactly how to build a specific workflow. Specificity is the targeting mechanism.

Give away the recipe. The people who want someone to cook it for them will find you.
posts.cta: Has a piece of content ever directly led to a deal for you? I'm collecting these stories.

remixes.label: inbound-is-outbound
remixes.hook:
I stopped sending cold emails three months ago.

My pipeline is up 40%.
remixes.body:
I stopped sending cold emails three months ago.

My pipeline is up 40%.

Not because cold email doesn't work. It does. But I realized I was spending 10 hours a week writing sequences when I could spend 3 hours writing content that does the same job at scale.

The math is simple. A cold email reaches one person. A tactical LinkedIn post about a specific Clay enrichment workflow reaches thousands. And when the right person sees it, they come to you. Pre-sold. Already trusting your expertise.

Here's what shifted for me. I stopped writing "thought leadership" and started writing technical breakdowns. Specific tools. Specific configurations. Specific results. The kind of post where someone screenshots it and sends it to their ops team.

Three deals in the last quarter came from content, not outbound. A VP shared my Clay breakdown internally. Their ops lead DM'd me. A founder bookmarked my automation post and reached out two weeks later when the need became urgent. A CRO commented on a post, we connected, now we're in a pilot.

None of these people would have opened a cold email from me. But they engaged with content that solved a problem they already had.

The best outbound strategy might not be outbound at all. It might be publishing the kind of specificity that makes prospects come to you.
remixes.cta: What's the best deal you've closed that started from a piece of content? I'm building a collection of these stories.

remixes.label: tactical-content-playbook
remixes.hook:
Your content strategy has an attribution problem.

Not a creation problem. An attribution problem.
remixes.body:
Your content strategy has an attribution problem.

Not a creation problem. An attribution problem.

Most B2B teams create plenty of content. The problem is they can't connect it to revenue, so leadership kills the budget every quarter.

I started tracking something simple. Every time someone books a call, I ask "what made you reach out?" Not a form field. A real conversation. And the answers are never what your CRM says.

"I saw your post about Clay workflows and sent it to my team."
"Your breakdown of that enrichment pipeline was exactly what we were trying to build."
"I've been following your posts for two months and finally had a project that fit."

None of these show up in your attribution model. HubSpot says they came from "direct" or "organic social." Which tells you nothing.

Here's what I've learned. The content that drives revenue isn't the content that gets the most likes. It's the content that gets screenshotted, forwarded to a Slack channel, and bookmarked for later. Tactical, specific, implementable.

Write the post that makes someone say "we need to talk to this person." That's outbound, just delivered through a feed instead of an inbox.

Stop measuring content by impressions. Start measuring it by conversations it creates.
remixes.cta: How do you actually track which content drives pipeline? I've tried five different approaches and I'm still iterating.

My recommendation: stop thinking about content and outbound as separate motions. They're the same thing. Content is just outbound that scales.

The key is specificity. Generic thought leadership gets likes. Technical breakdowns get forwarded to buying committees. Write the post that an ops lead screenshots and drops in their team Slack.

2. Write for the person who will forward it, not just the person who will like it.
3. Track dark social attribution. Ask every prospect "what made you reach out?" in conversation. Your CRM attribution is lying to you.

The best pipeline I've built in the last six months came from content, not sequences. The deals close faster because the prospect already trusts your expertise before the first call.

And yet most B2B companies still have their content team in one building and their outbound team in another. As if they're doing different things.

They're not. Content IS outbound. It's just outbound that doesn't make people hate you.

Think about it. A cold email interrupts someone's day to pitch them something they didn't ask for. A tactical LinkedIn post shows up in their feed, solves a real problem, and makes them reach out voluntarily. Same result. Completely different dynamic.

But the outbound industrial complex doesn't want you to think this way. Because if you realized that three hours of content creation per week could replace ten hours of sequence writing, a whole category of tools becomes unnecessary.

The companies still separating "content strategy" from "demand gen" are running a playbook from 2019. The best outbound reps I know are prolific content creators. The best content creators I know have pipelines that outbound teams envy.

Stop asking "should we invest in content or outbound?" It's the same thing. The only question is whether your content is specific enough to actually drive action.

posts.title: I automated myself out of my own job
posts.hook:
Six months ago I was the person enriching leads, writing sequences, and updating the CRM.

Now my cron jobs do it at midnight and I review the output over coffee.
posts.body:
Six months ago I was the person enriching leads, writing sequences, and updating the CRM.

Now my cron jobs do it at midnight and I review the output over coffee.

The weird part about being a GTM engineer is that half the job is automating yourself. Every manual process I touch, I immediately think "how do I make this run without me?"

Current stack that runs autonomously:
- Lead enrichment pipeline fires at 10pm, scores and routes by morning
- Daily blog post generated from trending AI news, published before I wake up
- Outbound data refresh runs weekly, flags stale records for review
- Content distribution pipeline takes one post and adapts it across 4 platforms

Each of these used to be a 2-3 hour weekly task. Combined, that's 8-12 hours a week I got back.

But here's what actually changed. I didn't use those hours to "scale." I used them to think. To build the next automation. To actually talk to prospects instead of updating spreadsheets.

The career pivot from operator to engineer isn't about doing less work. It's about doing different work. The repetitive stuff runs on machines. The creative, strategic, human stuff stays with me.

Daniel Vassallo talks about this with micro-SaaS. Build small things that run themselves. Same principle applies inside a GTM function. Build small automations that run themselves. Stack them. Compound the leverage.
posts.cta: What's one task you do every week that you know could be automated but haven't gotten to yet?

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel discusses his pivot from Amazon engineer to full-time indie developer, shipping micro-SaaS products like smallbets.me. He advocates for building in public to build audience and iterate fast, detailing how one product generated $10k MRR solo. Shares framework for tech career transitions.

remixes.label: midnight-employee
remixes.hook:
I have an employee who works from midnight to 6am, never complains, and costs me $0.003 per task.

It's a cron job.
remixes.body:
I have an employee who works from midnight to 6am, never complains, and costs me $0.003 per task.

It's a cron job.

Every night while I sleep, my automation stack processes the day's leads. Enriches company data from multiple sources. Scores them against my ICP. Writes personalized first lines. Updates the CRM. Generates a daily brief I read over coffee.

Six months ago, I did all of this manually. Took about 3 hours a day. Now it takes 10 minutes to review the output and make adjustments.

The strange thing about building these systems is the identity shift. You go from "person who does the work" to "person who designs the system that does the work." Same output. Completely different skill set.

What I've learned: the first automation saves you time. The second one saves you energy. By the third or fourth, you stop thinking in tasks and start thinking in systems. Every manual process becomes a design problem.

The uncomfortable part? You realize that a lot of what felt like "skilled work" was actually just pattern execution. The real skill was always in the decision-making, the strategy, the system design. The execution was just overhead we'd normalized.

I didn't automate myself out of a job. I automated myself into a better one.
remixes.cta: What's the first task you automated that made you realize "wait, why was I doing this manually?" I want to hear that moment.

remixes.label: automation-audit
remixes.hook:
I tracked every manual task I did for two weeks.

73% of my working hours were pattern execution a script could handle.
remixes.body:
I tracked every manual task I did for two weeks.

73% of my working hours were pattern execution a script could handle.

Here's how I ran the audit. Every time I did something, I logged it with three columns: task, time spent, and "could a script do this?" After two weeks I had a spreadsheet that was honestly embarrassing.

Lead enrichment: 45 minutes daily. Scriptable.
CRM updates: 30 minutes daily. Scriptable.
Sequence writing from templates: 40 minutes daily. Mostly scriptable.
Reporting pulls: 20 minutes daily. Completely scriptable.
Actual strategic thinking: maybe 2 hours a day.

So I started automating from the bottom. Lead enrichment first because it was the most mechanical. Then CRM updates. Then the reporting pulls. Each one took about a weekend to build and test.

Three months later, my calendar looks completely different. The mechanical work runs on cron jobs overnight. I wake up, review outputs for 15 minutes, make strategic decisions, and spend the rest of my day on work that actually requires a human brain.

The ROI isn't just time saved. It's cognitive load removed. When you're not context-switching between strategic thinking and data entry all day, the quality of your actual thinking improves dramatically.

The playbook: track everything for two weeks. Sort by "scriptable." Automate the most mechanical task first. Repeat.
remixes.cta: Try the two-week audit. Track every task and tag it scriptable or not. The number will surprise you. What do you think your percentage would be?

Two things to watch for. First, always build a review layer. Automation without human oversight creates expensive mistakes. Second, invest in monitoring. A broken cron job that runs silently wrong is worse than doing the task manually.

The goal isn't to remove yourself from the process. It's to move yourself to the part of the process where human judgment actually matters.

That's the part nobody wants to say out loud. A huge chunk of "skilled" GTM work is just pattern execution dressed up with a job title. Pull data from here. Transform it like this. Put it there. Repeat. It's not strategy. It's a for-loop with extra steps.

I automated my lead enrichment pipeline first. Then CRM updates. Then daily reporting. Then content scheduling. Each time I'd think "surely this one needs human judgment" and each time the automation did it fine. Better than fine, actually. More consistent. Fewer errors. No tired Fridays.

Here's what makes people uncomfortable: if you can write down the exact steps you follow to do a task, that task doesn't need you. It needs a script. You're just an expensive, inconsistent runtime.

The real question isn't "what can I automate?" It's "what am I doing that actually requires me to think?" Because that's your real job. Everything else is overhead you've been too busy to eliminate.

Stop being proud of being busy. Start being honest about which of your tasks are actually just scripts waiting to be written.

posts.title: LangChain vs just writing code
posts.hook:
Unpopular opinion: most AI agent frameworks add complexity you don't need.

I ripped LangChain out of a production pipeline last month and replaced it with 40 lines of Python.
posts.body:
Unpopular opinion: most AI agent frameworks add complexity you don't need.

I ripped LangChain out of a production pipeline last month and replaced it with 40 lines of Python.

The pipeline enriches leads with AI-generated company summaries. Old version: LangChain with chains, callbacks, output parsers, memory modules. 300+ lines across 4 files. Broke every time the library updated.

New version: direct API call to Claude, JSON response, write to database. 40 lines. One file. Hasn't broken once.

Frameworks are valuable when you need the abstraction. Multi-model routing, complex tool calling chains, conversation memory across sessions. Real problems that justify the dependency.

But most production AI code I see doesn't need any of that. It needs:
- Call an API
- Parse the response
- Do something with it

That's just... code. Regular code. You already know how to write it.

Swyx's point about agents moving from hype to practical deployment is exactly right. And practical deployment usually means simpler, not more complex. The teams shipping AI into production fastest are the ones with the fewest abstractions between their code and the model.

Before you pip install the next agent framework, ask: could I just write this directly? If the answer is yes, do that. You'll ship faster and debug easier.

Add the framework when the pain demands it. Not before.
posts.cta: What's the simplest AI integration you have in production right now?

remixes.label: framework-tax
remixes.hook:
Every AI framework you add to your stack comes with a tax.

The tax is paid in debugging hours when the abstraction breaks.
remixes.body:
Every AI framework you add to your stack comes with a tax.

The tax is paid in debugging hours when the abstraction breaks.

I learned this the hard way. Had a lead enrichment pipeline running on LangChain. Chains, callbacks, output parsers, memory modules. 300+ lines across 4 files. Worked great until it didn't.

Every library update broke something. The abstractions that saved me time on day one cost me double on day thirty. I spent more time reading LangChain changelogs than writing actual business logic.

So I ripped it out. Replaced the whole thing with 40 lines of Python. Direct API calls to Claude. A simple retry wrapper. JSON parsing with a fallback. That's it.

The pipeline has been running for months now. Zero framework-related breakages. When something goes wrong, I can read the entire codebase in two minutes and find the issue.

Here's the principle: abstractions should hide complexity you'll never need to touch. But with AI integrations, you almost always need to touch the complexity. You need to see the prompts. You need to read the responses. You need to understand why the model made a specific choice.

Frameworks that hide this from you aren't helping. They're just adding a layer between you and the thing you need to debug.

Before you add a framework, ask: does this save me time on the thing I'll spend the most time doing? Building is fast. Debugging and maintaining is where time goes. Optimize for that.
remixes.cta: What framework have you ripped out of a project and replaced with simpler code? I want to hear what broke the camel's back.

remixes.label: 40-lines-challenge
remixes.hook:
Challenge: take your most complex AI integration and rewrite it in under 50 lines.

I did this last month. Here's what I learned.
remixes.body:
Challenge: take your most complex AI integration and rewrite it in under 50 lines.

I did this last month. Here's what I learned.

My lead enrichment pipeline was 300+ lines spread across 4 files. LangChain chains, callbacks, output parsers, memory modules. It worked, but every time I needed to change something I'd spend 30 minutes just remembering how the abstractions fit together.

I rewrote it in a single file. 40 lines of Python. Here's roughly what it does:

- Pulls the company URL from the lead record
- Sends a structured prompt to Claude asking for a company summary, employee count estimate, and likely tech stack
- Parses the JSON response with a simple try/except fallback
- Writes the enriched data back to the CRM
- Retries on failure with exponential backoff

That's it. No chains. No callbacks. No output parsers. No memory modules. Just a function that takes input and produces output.

What I learned: most of the framework code wasn't solving my problem. It was solving hypothetical problems I never had. I didn't need conversation memory for a batch enrichment job. I didn't need streaming callbacks for a pipeline that runs at midnight. I didn't need output parsers when I could just parse JSON.

The rewrite took two hours. The maintenance time savings have been enormous. When it breaks, I read 40 lines and find the issue in minutes.

Simplicity isn't a compromise. For production AI integrations, it's a feature.
remixes.cta: Try the 50-line challenge on your most abstracted AI integration. What did the rewrite teach you about what you actually needed?

tones.advisor:
Most AI agent frameworks add complexity you don't need. I ripped LangChain out of a production pipeline last month and replaced it with 40 lines of Python. Here's why I'd recommend you consider doing the same.

The pipeline enriches leads with AI-generated company summaries. The old version used LangChain with chains, callbacks, output parsers, and memory modules. 300+ lines across 4 files. It broke every time the library updated.

The new version is a single file. Direct API call to Claude with a structured prompt. JSON parsing with a try/except fallback. Retry logic with exponential backoff. Write results to CRM. Done.

Here's my framework for deciding when you need a framework and when you don't.

Use a framework when: you need complex multi-agent orchestration, your pipeline has branching logic with many tool calls, or you're building something where the framework's abstractions directly map to your problem.

Skip the framework when: you're making straightforward API calls, your pipeline is essentially linear, you need to understand every decision the model makes, or you value maintenance simplicity over initial development speed.

Most production AI integrations I've seen fall into the second category. The framework was chosen because it felt like the "right" way to build, not because the problem required it.

My recommendation: start with the simplest possible implementation. Add abstraction layers only when you hit a specific problem that demands them. You'll be surprised how far raw API calls and basic Python will take you.
tones.provocateur:
Most AI agent frameworks add complexity you don't need. And the people building them know this. They just don't care because your complexity is their adoption metric.

I ripped LangChain out of a production pipeline last month. Replaced it with 40 lines of Python. The pipeline enriches leads with AI-generated company summaries. Old version: chains, callbacks, output parsers, memory modules. 300+ lines across 4 files. Broke constantly.

New version: one API call, one JSON parse, one retry wrapper. Runs flawlessly.

Here's what nobody in the framework ecosystem wants to admit. Most AI integrations are just API calls with some parsing logic. That's it. You send a prompt, you get a response, you do something with it. You don't need an "orchestration layer" for that. You need the requests library and ten minutes.

But frameworks sell conference talks. Frameworks create ecosystems. Frameworks justify VC funding rounds. So we get increasingly elaborate abstractions for problems that were already solved by basic programming.

The LangChain ecosystem has what, 200+ integrations now? Cool. My 40-line script has one integration. The one I actually use. And when it breaks, I can read the entire thing in two minutes instead of spelunking through abstraction layers trying to figure out which callback handler swallowed my error.

Stop letting framework hype dictate your architecture. Look at what you're actually building. If it's a linear pipeline with API calls, write it like one.

The new version is one file, one API call, one JSON parser, one retry wrapper. It's been running for a month without a single framework-related issue.

This week I want to walk through why simpler AI integrations are usually better ones, and when you actually do need the framework.

posts.title: RevOps alignment is a people problem not a tools problem
posts.hook:
Bought 6 tools to "align" sales, marketing, and CS.

Still had three teams with three dashboards measuring three different definitions of pipeline.
posts.body:
Bought 6 tools to "align" sales, marketing, and CS.

Still had three teams with three dashboards measuring three different definitions of pipeline.

I see this everywhere. Company decides they need RevOps alignment. Buys a BI tool. Builds dashboards. Creates a shared Slack channel. Declares alignment complete.

Three months later, marketing counts MQLs that sales doesn't recognize. Sales counts pipeline that CS says churns at 40%. CS measures health scores that nobody else looks at.

The tool worked fine. The definitions never got aligned.

Real RevOps alignment starts with one boring meeting where everyone agrees on:
- What counts as pipeline (specific stage, specific criteria)
- What counts as qualified (not "they downloaded a whitepaper")
- What counts as revenue (booked? invoiced? collected?)
- One dashboard. One source of truth. One set of numbers.

Then you wire the tools to enforce those definitions. CRM stages map to the agreed pipeline definition. Lead scoring reflects the agreed qualification criteria. Dashboards pull from one data model.

I've built these systems for teams. The technical work takes a week. Getting three department heads to agree on what "qualified" means takes a month.

The tools are the easy part. Always have been.
posts.cta: What's the one metric your sales and marketing teams still can't agree on?

remixes.label: shared-dictionary
remixes.hook:
Asked the head of sales what "pipeline" means.
Asked the CMO the same question.
Got two completely different answers.

That's your alignment problem right there.
remixes.body:
Asked the head of sales what "pipeline" means.
Asked the CMO the same question.
Got two completely different answers.

That's your alignment problem right there.

I've seen this at every company I've worked with. The teams aren't misaligned on goals. They're misaligned on definitions. Marketing says "we generated 500 MQLs." Sales says "we got 50 leads worth talking to." Same data. Different dictionaries.

No BI tool fixes this. No dashboard fixes this. No shared Slack channel fixes this.

What fixes it is sitting in a room and agreeing on exactly three things:

1. What counts as pipeline? Not the philosophical answer. The literal SQL query. If you can't write a WHERE clause that both teams agree on, you don't have alignment.

2. When does a lead become sales-ready? Not "when marketing says so." A specific set of observable criteria that both teams validated against closed-won deals.

3. What's the one number you both report to the CEO? Not two numbers that "tell the same story." One number. If you can't agree on one number, you're not aligned.

I've watched companies spend six figures on RevOps tools while their sales and marketing teams literally couldn't agree on what a qualified lead looks like. The tool wasn't the problem. The conversation that never happened was the problem.

Alignment isn't a software category. It's a shared dictionary. Build that first.
remixes.cta: What's the definition your sales and marketing teams disagree on most? I bet it's something that sounds simple but nobody's actually written down.

remixes.hook:
Graveyard of tools I've seen companies buy to "fix alignment":

$40K BI platform. Unused after month two.
$25K attribution tool. Sales doesn't trust the data.
$15K meeting scheduler. Didn't solve the actual problem.

The actual problem was a 30-minute conversation that never happened.
remixes.body:
Graveyard of tools I've seen companies buy to "fix alignment":

$40K BI platform. Unused after month two.
$25K attribution tool. Sales doesn't trust the data.
$15K meeting scheduler. Didn't solve the actual problem.

The actual problem was a 30-minute conversation that never happened.

Here's the pattern. Revenue team has friction. Leadership diagnoses it as a "tools problem." They evaluate vendors, run demos, sign contracts. Three months later the friction is exactly the same because the root cause was never technical.

The root cause is almost always one of three things:

1. Teams use different definitions for the same terms. Marketing's "pipeline" and sales's "pipeline" are different numbers from different queries. Nobody ever reconciled them.

2. Handoff criteria are vibes-based. Marketing "qualifies" leads using criteria that sales never agreed to. So sales ignores MQLs and both teams blame each other.

3. Teams optimize for different metrics. Marketing optimizes for volume. Sales optimizes for conversion. Both can succeed on their metrics while the company fails on revenue.

Every one of these is solved by conversation, not software. Get both teams in a room. Define terms. Agree on handoff criteria. Pick one shared metric.

Then buy the tool to measure it. Not the other way around.

I've seen more alignment come from a single whiteboard session than from any six-figure platform purchase.
remixes.cta: What's the most expensive tool your company bought to solve a problem that was actually just a communication gap?

tones.advisor:
I see this everywhere. Company decides they need RevOps alignment. They buy a BI tool. Build dashboards. Create a shared Slack channel. Declare alignment complete.

Three months later, marketing counts MQLs that sales doesn't recognize. Sales counts pipeline that marketing can't influence. Both teams report success on their own metrics while revenue stays flat.

Here's what I'd recommend instead. Before you buy any tool or build any dashboard, get alignment on three foundational questions.

First, shared definitions. What exactly is a qualified lead? Not the marketing definition or the sales definition. The one definition both teams will use. Write it as a literal set of criteria. If you can't, you're not aligned yet.

Second, handoff standards. What observable signals trigger a marketing-to-sales handoff? Validate these against your last 20 closed-won deals. If the criteria don't match what actually converted, they're wrong.

Third, one shared metric. Not two metrics that correlate. One number that both teams own together. Revenue from marketing-sourced pipeline is a good starting point.

Once you have these three things documented and agreed upon, then choose the tools to measure and operationalize them. The tools become dramatically more effective when they're measuring something everyone actually agrees on.

Alignment is a people process first and a technology problem second. Most companies have it backwards.
tones.provocateur:
Bought 6 tools to "align" sales, marketing, and CS. Still had three teams with three dashboards measuring three different definitions of pipeline.

Because alignment isn't a product you can buy. And the RevOps vendor ecosystem really hopes you never figure that out.

Think about how ridiculous this is. A company spends $80K on BI platforms and attribution tools to "create alignment" between teams that have never once sat in a room and agreed on what the word "pipeline" means. The tool works perfectly. It accurately measures three different versions of reality depending on who's looking at it.

The real alignment problem takes 30 minutes and a whiteboard to solve. Get sales and marketing in the same room. Ask them both to define "qualified lead." Watch the chaos unfold. Then don't leave until you have one answer.

But nobody sells a 30-minute whiteboard session for $40K per year. So instead we get an entire software category dedicated to putting dashboards on top of disagreements. Making misalignment look professional with nice charts.

I've watched companies cycle through three or four "alignment tools" in two years while the underlying problem never changes. The VP of Sales still doesn't trust marketing's lead numbers. Marketing still can't explain why their MQLs don't convert. And now they have a really expensive dashboard showing them exactly how misaligned they are in real time.

Have the conversation first. Buy the tool after.

platforms.newsletter:
I want to talk about something that drives me a little crazy in RevOps.

Every company I work with has the same story. They bought tools to create alignment between sales and marketing. Dashboards, attribution platforms, shared workspaces. And three months later, marketing is still counting MQLs that sales ignores, sales is still counting pipeline that marketing can't influence, and both teams are reporting success on their own metrics.

The tools work fine. The problem is that nobody ever agreed on what they're measuring. This week I want to break down why alignment is a vocabulary problem first and a technology problem second.
